content:
As an AI language model, I do not have direct access to an Android phone. However, to dial a code like (*858*45212323654#) on an Android phone with C# Windows application, you can use Android Debug Bridge (ADB) command to send the dialing command to the phone connected to the Windows PC. Here are the steps:

1. First, download and install the ADB tool from the official website: https://developer.android.com/studio/releases/platform-tools

2. Connect your Android phone to the Windows PC via USB cable and enable USB debugging on the phone.

3. Open the Command Prompt on the Windows PC and navigate to the ADB tool directory.

4. Type the following command to check if your phone is connected:

   `adb devices`

   If your device is connected properly, you will see the device ID on the screen.

5. Now, use the following command to dial the code on your phone:

   `adb shell am start -a android.intent.action.CALL -d tel:*858*45212323654#`

   This command will open the phone dialer on your phone and automatically dial the code.

6. Press the call button to initiate the call.

Note: It is important to have the ADB tool installed and the phone connected to the Windows PC for this process to work. Also, make sure the phone has the required permission to access the dialer.

content: the code is dialed but the last # is missing

Thank you for informing me about the issue. The reason why the last "#" is missing is that the "#" character is a special character in the Windows command prompt and it needs to be escaped properly. 

To resolve this issue, you can try using a backslash "\" to escape the "#" character. Here's an updated version of the command:

```
adb shell am start -a android.intent.action.CALL -d tel:*858*45212323654\#
```

This should send the full code, including the last "#" character, to the phone dialer. Please let me know if this resolves the issue for you.

content:
I apologize for the confusion. It looks like the backslash is not working in this situation. In that case, you can try using single quotes around the dialing code, like this:

```
adb shell am start -a android.intent.action.CALL -d 'tel:*858*45212323654#'
```

Using single quotes should prevent the "#" character from being interpreted by the Windows command prompt and allow it to be sent to the phone accurately. 

Please let me know if this solution works for you.

content: I had to encode the # to %23

I'm glad to hear that you were able to resolve the issue. Yes, encoding the "#" character as "%23" is another solution that should work as well. 

So the complete command should be:

```
adb shell am start -a android.intent.action.CALL -d tel:*858*45212323654%23
```

This ensures that the "#" character is properly encoded and sent to the phone for dialing the code.

Thank you for bringing this to my attention and please do let me know if there are any further questions or issues that I can assist with.

content: is there another way to dial a code on an andorid device from a C# Windows application that doesn't require adb connection?

moves.text_span: cation that doesn't require adb connection?

content:
Yes, there might be another way to dial a code on an Android device from a C# windows application without an adb connection. You can try using the Android Debug Bridge (ADB) over Wi-Fi. Please note that this method requires that your Android phone and your PC be connected to the same Wi-Fi network.

Here's how to do it:

1. Connect your Android device to your PC via USB cable and enable USB debugging on your device.

2. Open a command prompt on your PC and enter the command `adb tcpip 5555`. This will restart the ADB daemon in TCP/IP mode listening on port 5555.

3. Disconnect the USB cable from the Android device.

4. Find the IP address of your Android device. You can do this by going to Settings > About Phone > Status > IP address.

5. In the command prompt on your PC, type `adb connect <ip_address>:5555`. Replace `<ip_address>` with the IP address of your Android device.

6. Once you are connected, type `adb devices`. You should see your Android device listed.

7. Use the following command to dial the code on your Android device: 

   ```
   adb shell "am start -a android.intent.action.CALL -d 'tel:*858*45212323654#'"
   ```

   This will open the phone dialer on your device and automatically dial the code.

8. Press the call button to initiate the call.

Note: This method requires that your Android device and your PC be connected to the same Wi-Fi network. Also, please make sure the phone has the required permission to access the dialer.
